findings.description: find_user_by_email runs 'SELECT * FROM users', fetching and materializing the ENTIRE users table to find one email, instead of pushing a 'WHERE email = ?' filter to the DB; full-table-scan + N object allocations for a single-row lookup.

After fetching every row, the code does a linear O(n) Python scan over all_users to match one email — work that an indexed WHERE clause would do in O(log n) inside the DB; latency and CPU grow linearly with table size.

all_users list comprehension eagerly builds a full in-memory list of User objects for the whole table on top of fetchall(); unbounded memory proportional to user count, with no early exit or streaming.

self._sessions is an unbounded in-memory cache with no max size and no automatic TTL eviction; cleanup_expired must be invoked manually (nothing schedules it) and the inverted validate_session check neuters lazy eviction, so memory grows with login volume.
Every public DB method opens a fresh sqlite3 connection per call with no pooling/reuse; under web-service load this repeats connection setup (file open, cold page cache) on every login and lookup.

login uses 'SELECT *' and relies on email equality; if the email column is unindexed each login is a full scan — conditional on schema which is out of scope, hence low confidence (also a SQL-injection vector — defer to critic-security).

cleanup_expired builds an intermediate 'stale' list then iterates to delete; for very large session maps this is an extra O(n) allocation pass, though acceptable to avoid mutating-while-iterating — minor.
Not a perf issue: _hash_password's 100k-iteration PBKDF2 is intentional security cost, not a bottleneck to optimize away — flagged only to pre-empt a wrong 'slow hash' critique.
